input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware architecture consists of lower‑level and upper‑level chassis structures together with dedicated autonomy elements (perception, planning and actuation modules). The vehicle operates in a test‑track environment where an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) function is exercised, making it a suitable platform for digital twinning because its on‑board electronic control units are resource‑constrained and can off‑load computation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three main services: (1) Data Monitoring – a real‑time UI that visualises vehicle state, projected driving path and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save and generate reports of logged data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, speed‑setpoint calculation) runs in the twin (e.g., in the cloud) and the resulting desired speed is sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI enables operators to enable/disable ACC, set the desired speed manually, and issue an emergency‑brake command. All services require low‑latency end‑to‑end communication, data collection and reporting capabilities, and are used by operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting and data analysis).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware for communication and data exchange, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the software development kit providing GPU‑accelerated compute capabilities, and MATLAB/Simulink for modelling and simulation of complex control algorithms. Collected vehicle data are persisted in an SQL database (as referenced in Table I). These tools together enable the low‑latency, cloud‑based execution of ACC and the interactive UI used for monitoring and remote management.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Clock synchronization between the physical vehicle and its digital twin must achieve an accuracy of 100 ms, as required for safety‑critical vehicular applications and limited by the hardware; this shared notion of time enables computation offloading across devices.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system consists of a single instance of three hierarchical entities defined by ISO 23247: the Physical Twin Entity (host of compute, sensors, actuators), the Digital Twin Entity (data management and control), and the User Entity (visualisation and control‑panel UI). Each entity is deployed on a distinct hardware component, resulting in a decentralized architecture with one Functional Entity per ROS2 package; no multiple twin instances are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture instantiated with ROS 2: each Functional Entity is packaged as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes; communication between entities occurs via ROS 2 topics/services, enabling coordination across the separate hardware platforms. The mapping of software components to entities provides resource‑level segregation and ownership.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates a behavioral tracking model named “Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking” that applies DBSCAN clustering on incoming point‑cloud data followed by a Kalman filter to maintain object tracks. Geometric data is supplied by the LiDAR and depth‑camera sensors, while analytical processing is performed by the clustering algorithm. All generated and raw data are persisted in a relational SQL database, and a dedicated Data Translation component serializes objects for transmission between functional entities. Model relationships are defined such that raw sensor streams feed the clustering module, whose output is consumed by the tracking module and stored for later analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical twin includes a drive Motor, a Steering Servo, and a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (ESC). These actuators are controlled via the Data Collection and Device Control Entity and expose remote command interfaces that satisfy Requirement R5 (emergency braking) and Requirement R6 (speed and steering set‑point control). Actuation ranges encompass full motor speed range, servo angular limits for steering, and ESC current limits. Safety constraints enforce an immediate stop on emergency brake commands, and control messages are addressed to each actuator through their network identifiers.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing is performed by a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR unit and an Intel Deepsense D435i depth camera. The LiDAR provides 2‑D range measurements used for object detection, while the depth camera supplies 3‑D point clouds for clustering and tracking. Together they enable real‑time acquisition of vehicle speed, steering angle, and surrounding object tracks as specified in Requirement R2. Data are streamed from the sensors to the Data Collection entity for further processing.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, motor status, steering servo position, vehicle state) is collected on the physical twin and transferred to the Digital Twin over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP transport. The raw point‑cloud stream is first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component (Data Pre‑Processing FE) which extracts object tracks. All streams are then handled by the Data Translation functional entity (ISO 23247 Data Translation FE) which serialises the messages, aligns the data models and performs unit conversions before publishing them on the ROS 2 middleware used by the DT services. Communication between the DT software components occurs inside a ROS 2 network. No explicit transmission frequency, event‑trigger, or quantitative data‑quality assurance details are provided.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are sent from the Digital Twin back to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi UDP channel. Commands include enabling/disabling ACC, setting a desired speed, and an emergency‑brake command; these are issued by the remote‑management UI and by the off‑loaded ACC computation (which calculates a desired speed based on lead‑vehicle distance). The commands are serialized and unit‑converted by the Data Translation FE before transmission. The UI displays confirmation of command execution, but the documents do not describe specific validation, authentication, acknowledgement, or conflict‑resolution mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑component communication within the digital twin system is built on ROS 2. Communication between the physical twin and the digital twin occurs over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP transport. The system is required to provide low end‑to‑end latency for safety‑critical ACC functionality, but the documents do not give quantitative bandwidth or latency targets, nor do they describe redundancy, reliability mechanisms, or specific security protocols for the network.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DT services are executed on external compute resources that can be “better‑suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud”, enabling off‑loading of the ACC algorithm. No details are given about the precise hosting platform (cloud provider, edge or on‑premise), required computational or storage capacities, scalability mechanisms, supported deployment models, or availability/reliability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The ISO 23247 framework structures the Digital Twin lifecycle into four sequential parts: (1) Overview and General Principles – defines the conceptual stage and high‑level goals; (2) Reference Architecture – establishes the entity‑based architectural stage where the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity are defined; (3) Digital Representation of Physical Manufacturing Elements – implements the Digital Twin Entity using functional entities that model the OME; (4) Information Exchange – integrates the DT with external systems and supports the transition to operational use. Governance of the lifecycle is expressed through adherence to the ISO 23247 standard, which prescribes the entities, functional entities and their responsibilities, but does not detail transition mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 development framework. Designers decompose the DT into the four entities defined by the reference architecture and further break each entity into Functional Entities (FEs) that perform tasks such as actuation, data transformation, or user interfacing. The document cites OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as a possible technology for implementing FEs, indicating that the DT may be built on service‑oriented, standardized communication protocols. Evolution of the DT is managed by updating the functional entities or adding new ones while staying within the ISO 23247 entity model, but the document does not provide details on version‑control, testing, or quality‑assurance procedures.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system complies with ISO 23247 (four‑part Digital Twin framework) and adopts its entity‑based reference architecture. Additional standards cited in the broader document include ISO 15622 for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) implementation and 3GPP Technical Specification 22.185 for network latency requirements. Interoperability is supported through the optional use of OPC UA, aligning the DT with common industrial communication standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

It correctly details the hardware architecture, specifically mentioning the 'lower level chassis' and 'upper level chassis' along with autonomy elements. It also correctly identifies the motivation for digital twinning as the resource-constrained nature of on-board ECUs. However, the extraction mentions a 'test‑track environment', which is not explicitly found in the source text (the text refers to a 'real-world setting'), preventing a perfect score.

The extraction is comprehensive and highly specific. It perfectly matches the three services defined in Section III.A: 'Data Monitoring' (visualizing state, path, tracks, reporting), 'Computation Offloading' (running ACC in the twin/cloud to calculate desired speed), and 'Remote Management' (enabling/disabling ACC, setting speed, emergency braking). It also correctly identifies the users ('operators and developers') and the need for 'low end-to-end latency'.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific tools (ROS2, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0, MATLAB/Simulink, SQL database) and their roles as middleware, SDK, modeling tool, and storage, respectively. Supported by quotes: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', and 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately details the 100 ms clock synchronization requirement, the necessity of a shared notion of time for computation offloading, and the context of safety-critical applications and hardware limitations. This is strongly supported by the source text.

The extraction correctly identifies the three hierarchical entities (Physical, Digital, User) defined by ISO 23247, their specific roles, the deployment on distinct hardware components (decentralized), and the mapping to ROS2 packages.

The description is correct regarding the instantiation of ISO 23247 with ROS 2, the packaging of Functional Entities, and the impact on ownership. However, the text specifies communication occurs via a 'ROS2 network' and 'Wi-Fi', whereas the extraction specifies 'ROS 2 topics/services'. While likely true for ROS 2, 'topics/services' is not explicitly in the text, warranting a slight deduction.

The source text focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (Physical, Digital, and User entities). While it mentions 'cloud-based fleet coordination' as a general need and 'offloading' computation, it does not describe specific data exchange protocols, interoperability standards, or integration patterns with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins as defined by the characteristic.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a detailed and accurate description of the data flow, correctly identifying the input data types (LiDAR, motor, steering, vehicle state), the specific software components (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Data Translation FE), and the internal ROS 2 communication. It correctly notes the absence of specific transmission frequencies. However, the claim that the link uses 'UDP transport' is not fully supported by the provided text, which is truncated at 'using U', requiring external knowledge to confirm 'UDP'.

The description accurately lists the control commands (enable/disable ACC, speed, emergency brake) and their sources (UI and off-loaded ACC computation). It correctly identifies the role of the Data Translation FE and the lack of security details. However, it relies on the unsupported 'UDP' protocol claim (text truncated at 'using U') and asserts that the 'UI displays confirmation of command execution', which is not explicitly stated in the provided text (the text only mentions enabling management).

The description correctly identifies ROS 2 for internal communication and Wi-Fi with UDP for the physical-digital twin link. It accurately notes the requirement for low end-to-end latency ('at most 100 ms') due to the safety-critical nature of ACC, but correctly states that quantitative bandwidth targets or specific security protocols are not provided in the text. Supported by quotes: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U', 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity'.

The extraction is excellent and perfectly grounded in the source text. It accurately captures the strategy of off-loading computation to 'better-suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud' and correctly identifies that specific implementation details (provider, capacity, scalability) are absent from the documents.

_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)

It correctly frames the standard as providing the structural framework for the lifecycle. However, it focuses primarily on the standard's structure rather than the specific development phases (e.g., design, operation) mentioned elsewhere in the text, so it lacks a bit of depth on the actual case study phases.

The extraction provides a detailed and precise description of the engineering methodology, correctly noting the decomposition of the DT into entities and Functional Entities (FEs) and the mapping of software components to these FEs. It accurately cites OPC UA as a potential implementation technology mentioned in Section II.A and correctly identifies the lack of specific QA or version-control procedures in the text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the document describes services like 'Data Monitoring' and 'Remote Management', it does not describe 'types of insights generated', specific 'analytics and processing capabilities' (beyond control logic), or 'confidence measures' as requested by the characteristic description. The text mentions reporting 'relevant information of the ADAS decision-making process' as a requirement (R4), but does not detail the decision support capabilities themselves.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific standards mentioned in the document for the architecture (ISO 23247), the control logic (ISO 15622), and network requirements (3GPP). It also accurately notes the optional use of OPC UA for communication. Supported by quotes: 'The system shall implement ACC, as outlined in ISO 15622' and 'This figure comes from the 3rd Generation Partnership Plan (3GPP) Technical Specification, 22.185' and 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation...'
